source_quote: There were no differences in the incidence of postoperative fever (18.7% vs. 21.5%, P=0.66) and transfusion (12.5% vs. 20%, P=0.19) versus between two group.
reasoning: The outcome is 'Requirement for blood transfusion'. The text states 'transfusion (12.5% vs. 20%, P=0.19)' comparing cesarean myomectomy group vs. cesarean section without myomectomy. From Table 3 context, cesarean myomectomy group n=65, control group n=96. Events calculated as: 12.5% of 65 = 8.125 ≈ 8; 20% of 96 = 19.2 ≈ 19.

source_quote: Falls incidence: 0.77±0.44 [pre] to 0.0±0.0*** [post] in exercise intervention group (n=11); 0.93±0.3 [pre] to 0.8±0.4 [post] in control group (n=13). ***P<0.001, significant difference from pre-training values
reasoning: The outcome is 'Rate of falls sensitivity analysis: active exercise including Cadore 2014'. Table 1 shows falls incidence (mean±SD) post-training: intervention 0.0±0.0 (n=11), control 0.8±0.4 (n=13). This is reported as a continuous measure (falls incidence rate), not binary event counts. Sample sizes are explicitly stated in the table.

source_quote: In the per-protocol population (n = 1894), the incidence of RSV-associated ARI occurring ≥14 days after dosing was 1.7% and 1.6% in the vaccine and placebo groups, respectively
reasoning: The outcome is 'RSV-associated lower respiratory tract infection'. The text only provides percentages (1.7% vaccine, 1.6% placebo) without stating whether n=1894 is the total population or per-group. The individual group sample sizes and event counts are not explicitly stated, only the combined per-protocol population. The outcome is 'All-cause medically attended acute respiratory illnesses (MAARI)'. The results text discusses RSV vaccine shedding, infection rates, and antibody responses, but does not report MAARI event counts. No explicit data on medically attended acute respiratory illnesses is provided.

source_quote: During 28 days postinoculation, upper respiratory illness and/or fever occurred in 64% of RSV/ΔNS2/Δ1313/I1314L, 84% of RSV/276, and 58% of placebo recipients.
reasoning: The outcome is 'All-cause medically attended acute respiratory illnesses (MAARI)'. The text reports upper respiratory illness and/or fever percentages but: (1) these are not explicitly described as 'medically attended', (2) this is a 3-arm trial with two different vaccine types, making it unclear which intervention to compare, (3) the exact sample sizes per arm are not stated (only total n=34 vaccine, n=16 placebo), and (4) event counts would require calculation from unstated denominators. Cannot extract without making multiple assumptions.

source_quote: Table 4 shows genital tract trauma outcomes. [...] Of women who experienced trauma, 242 (20%) had major trauma (defined as a second, third, or fourth degree perineal laceration; a laceration in the mid- or inner vaginal vault; or a cervical laceration)
reasoning: The outcome is '2nd degree tears'. The text mentions that major trauma included 'second, third, or fourth degree perineal laceration' occurring in 242 women (20%) total, but this is combined across all three intervention groups and aggregates 2nd, 3rd, and 4th degree tears together. Table 4 is referenced but not provided in the excerpt. No group-specific counts for 2nd degree tears alone are explicitly stated.

The outcome is 'All-cause medically attended acute respiratory illnesses (MAARI)'. The results text discusses respiratory symptoms and fever rates (60% vaccinees and 27% placebo recipients) during the 28-day post-vaccination period, but these are not medically attended acute respiratory illnesses. The text mentions '5 of 19 vaccinees had ≥4-fold increases in antibody titers postsurveillance without RSV-MAARI' which indicates MAARI was tracked, but no event counts for MAARI are explicitly stated.

source_quote: In the CRYO and control groups, respectively, other outcomes included hysterectomy in four (10.5%) and zero (0%) patients
